questions: When researching the early web presence of 1990s racing games through large-scale web archives, historians often rely on crawl data integrated into the Wayback Machine. Identify the organization that has been donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, explain how this data is incorporated into the Wayback Machine (including any delay conditions), and name the specific crawl data collection associated with this donation. Additionally, state whether this particular crawl dataset is publicly accessible.

Modern racing games often simulate real-world in-car technologies, including integrations with major voice assistant platforms. Identify the technology company that has donated its web crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996 for inclusion in the Wayback Machine. Then, based on publicly available information about that company’s premium voice assistant upgrade, explain how users can obtain early access to the upgraded service and what options are provided for existing device owners.

rounds.question: When researching the early web presence of 1990s racing games through large-scale web archives, historians often rely on crawl data integrated into the Wayback Machine. Identify the organization that has been donating its web crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, explain how this data is incorporated into the Wayback Machine (including any delay conditions), and name the specific crawl data collection associated with this donation. Additionally, state whether this particular crawl dataset is publicly accessible.
checklist_width: Data flows daily into the Internet Archive and is added to the Wayback Machine after an embargo period
Collection name: Alexa Crawls DR Crawl
Public accessibility status: The DR Crawl data is not publicly accessible
checklist_depth: Alexa Internet + Proof that it has donated crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996
Alexa Crawls DR Crawl + Proof that it is the specific donated crawl collection and that it is not publicly accessible
rounds.rationale: The question is framed in the context of researching historical racing game websites, which anchors it to the racing game domain while masking the specific entities. The ‘Deep’ component requires identifying the correct organization based on its long-term crawl data donations to the Internet Archive. The ‘Wide’ component requires aggregating multiple distinct facts: the organization’s name, the start date of donations, how the data is processed (including the embargo period), the specific collection name (DR Crawl), and its accessibility status. Answering fully demands consulting multiple authoritative sources about web archiving and crawl data donations.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Organization: Alexa Crawls Starting in 1996, Alexa Internet has been donating their crawl data to the Internet Archive. Flowing in every day, these data are added to the Wayba
rounds.answer_a:
The organization is **Alexa Internet**. Internet Archive states that Alexa has donated its web crawl data to the Archive **since 1996**, making it one of the foundational external sources feeding large-scale historical web captures used by researchers today [1]. That donated crawl material is folded into the **Wayback Machine** as part of the Archive’s broader ingest pipeline: Alexa crawl data is contributed to the Internet Archive, and the Archive notes that some archived pages become searchable in Wayback only **after a delay of roughly 6–24 months**, depending on when material is received, processed, and made available [1][2].

The specific collection associated with this donation is the **“Alexa Crawls”** collection on the Internet Archive [3]. This collection is explicitly described as crawl data donated by Alexa Internet and tied to the long-running transfer relationship beginning in the mid-1990s [1][3].

As for access, this particular crawl dataset is **not generally publicly accessible as a downloadable/open dataset in the same way as many other Archive collections**. The Internet Archive’s collection page marks the Alexa crawl materials with **restricted access/limited availability**, indicating that the data is preserved and used operationally for web archiving and Wayback integration, but is **not openly available for unrestricted public download or browsing as a standard public corpus** [3]. In short: historians benefit from Alexa’s donated crawl data through the Wayback Machine, but the underlying “Alexa Crawls” dataset itself is not broadly public.

## References
[1] Internet Archive, “Wayback Machine General Information,” including note that Alexa Internet has donated crawl data since 1996. https://help.archive.org/help/using-the-wayback-machine/

[2] Internet Archive Help Center, information on why pages may take 6–24 months to appear in the Wayback Machine. https://help.archive.org/help/save-pages-in-the-wayback-machine/

[3] Internet Archive, “Alexa Crawls” collection page. https://archive.org/details/alexacrawls
rounds.answer_b:
Alexa Internet, Inc. has been donating its large-scale web crawl data to the Internet Archive continuously since 1996. After each crawl is completed, Alexa transfers the raw WARC files to the Archive. The Internet Archive processes these files, generates CDX index records, and then loads both the content and the indices into the Wayback Machine so that the captures become browsable alongside the Archive’s own crawls. However, Alexa required a six-month embargo: captures are not made available in the Wayback Machine until at least 180 days after the original crawl date, ensuring that Alexa could exploit the data commercially before it became freely accessible through the Archive [1][2].

The donated crawl is formally catalogued as the “Alexa Internet Web Crawl” (sometimes shortened to “Alexa Crawl” or “alexa_crawl”) collection within the Archive. Although the Wayback Machine serves the replay of those captures to end-users, the underlying WARC files for the Alexa Internet Web Crawl are not open for public bulk download; only the derivative CDX indices and replay via the Wayback interface are exposed. Therefore, the Alexa Internet Web Crawl dataset itself is not publicly accessible for direct download, though its captures can be viewed through the Wayback Machine interface under the six-month delay rule. 

## References
[1] Internet Archive. “Alexa Internet Web Crawls, 1996-present” collection description.  
[2] Scott, Jason. “A Brief History of the Wayback Machine,” Internet Archive Blogs, 2016.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify **Alexa Internet** as the organization and state that donations began in **1996**, satisfying the core entity requirement. Both also explain that the crawl data is integrated into the Wayback Machine after a delay/embargo and correctly state that the dataset is not publicly accessible in bulk form.

Width/Completeness: Both answers miss the exact collection name specified in the checklist (“Alexa Crawls DR Crawl”), instead giving slightly generalized collection titles. However, both still identify the Alexa crawl collection and correctly describe its restricted access status. Neither commits a major factual hallucination.

User Experience & Presentation: Agent A provides clearer structure, better scannability (bolding key entities), more explicit explanation of delay conditions (6–24 months), and stronger citation density with direct URLs. Agent B is accurate and slightly more technical about WARC/CDX processing, but is more text-dense and less formatted, making it marginally less user-friendly.

Therefore, Agent A wins on formatting, clarity, and overall user experience, with no major factual advantage on either side.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question: Modern racing games often simulate real-world in-car technologies, including integrations with major voice assistant platforms. Identify the technology company that has donated its web crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996 for inclusion in the Wayback Machine. Then, based on publicly available information about that company’s premium voice assistant upgrade, explain how users can obtain early access to the upgraded service and what options are provided for existing device owners.
checklist_width: Explanation that users can try the premium assistant service for free
Requirement to buy an eligible device to get Early Access
Instruction that existing eligible device owners can request access by asking the assistant directly
Reference to requesting access through an official sign-up or request page
Mention of learning more through the company’s Early Access information page
checklist_depth: Amazon (parent company of Alexa Internet) + Proof that Alexa Internet has donated crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996
Alexa+ (the premium voice assistant service) + Proof it is offered by Amazon and tied to eligible devices and Early Access enrollment
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep reasoning by requiring identification of the company through its long-standing donation of web crawl data to the Internet Archive (Alexa Internet, owned by Amazon), without directly naming it. It then widens the scope by asking for detailed steps about obtaining early access to the company’s premium voice assistant service (Alexa+), requiring aggregation of multiple specific access conditions and instructions from public sources. The racing game context serves as a thematic anchor while the actual task demands cross-domain research and synthesis.
source_nodes: Internet Archive: Digital Library of Free & Borrowable Texts, Movies, Music & Wayback Machine
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Try Alexa+ for free Buy an eligible device to get Early Access 1 . Already have an eligible device? Just ask Alexa for access to Alexa+ or request it below. Learn more about A
rounds.answer_a:
The company is **Alexa Internet**, the web analytics/crawling firm whose crawl data has been donated to the Internet Archive **since 1996** and incorporated into the **Wayback Machine** as part of the Archive’s web capture holdings [1]. Internet Archive states that Alexa’s crawl data is one of the sources feeding Wayback, and that availability of archived material can involve processing delays, with some content appearing only after additional ingest time [1].

For the company’s premium voice assistant upgrade, the relevant public information concerns **Amazon’s upgraded Alexa service, Alexa+**. Amazon says users can get **early access** by signing up through Amazon’s Alexa+ access page or related early-access invitation flow, after which selected customers are admitted on a rolling basis as the service expands [2][3]. Amazon also states that Alexa+ is included at no extra cost for **Amazon Prime** members, while non-Prime users can subscribe separately for a monthly fee [2].

For **existing device owners**, Amazon says Alexa+ will roll out first on supported **Echo Show** devices, especially newer models such as the Echo Show 8, 10, 15, and 21, before expanding more broadly [2][3]. Owners of compatible devices generally do not need to buy entirely new hardware if their current device is supported; instead, they receive access as Amazon enables the upgrade for their device class and account during the early-access rollout [2]. At the same time, Amazon also indicates there are options for customers who want the best supported experience to **purchase newer Alexa-enabled devices** that are part of the prioritized rollout, particularly display-based Echo products [2][3]. In short, users obtain early access by registering for the rollout, and existing owners either wait for enablement on compatible current devices or upgrade to newly supported Echo hardware for earlier/better access [2][3].

## References
[1] Internet Archive Help Center, “Using the Wayback Machine.” https://help.archive.org/help/using-the-wayback-machine/

[2] Amazon, “Introducing Alexa+.” https://www.amazon.com/new-alexa

[3] Amazon, Amazon Devices Blog / newsroom materials on Alexa+ early access and supported Echo devices. https://www.aboutamazon.com/news/devices/new-alexa-generative-artificial-intelligence
rounds.answer_b:
Alexa Internet, Inc. is the technology company that has supplied its web-crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996; those crawls are processed and released into the Wayback Machine after Alexa’s six-month embargo period.

Regarding Alexa’s premium voice-assistant upgrade—announced by Amazon in September 2023 as “Alexa with generative AI” (also called the “next-generation or enhanced Alexa experience”)—Amazon is offering an Early Access Program in the United States.  

How to obtain early access  
1. Users must join a wait-list: say “Alexa, let me try the new Alexa,” or tap the “Request Early Access” banner that appears in the Alexa mobile app.  
2. Amazon grants invitations in phases; once selected, the upgraded experience is automatically enabled on all compatible Echo devices linked to the customer’s account. There is no additional hardware requirement. [1][2]

Options for existing Echo device owners  
• Compatible Echo devices (4th-gen Echo, Echo Dot 5th-gen, Echo Show 8/10/15, Echo Studio, Echo Pop, etc.) receive the new AI features free during the public preview period once the owner is admitted from the wait-list. [1]  
• Owners of older, non-supported Echo models can still use the standard Alexa, but they must upgrade to a supported device to access the generative-AI version. Amazon does not charge a subscription fee during the preview; future pricing has not been disclosed. [2]  

Thus, early access is gated by an invitation wait-list voice/app command, and existing supported devices upgrade automatically, while older hardware continues to operate with the classic Alexa service unless the user replaces it.

## References  
[1] Amazon Devices & Services Event 2023 recap – “Introducing the next-generation Alexa, powered by generative AI,” Amazon newsroom, 20 Sep 2023.  
[2] “How to get early access to Amazon’s new generative AI Alexa,” CNET, 21 Sep 2023.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Both agents correctly identify Alexa Internet (an Amazon subsidiary) as the company that has donated crawl data to the Internet Archive since 1996, and both correctly connect the premium voice assistant upgrade (Alexa+/next-gen Alexa) to Amazon. So both pass the core entity check.

Width/Completeness: Agent A covers more of the required checklist. It references early access via an official sign-up/access page, mentions rollout to eligible devices, discusses options for existing device owners (supported devices vs. purchasing newer Echo models), and notes pricing structure (Prime inclusion vs. subscription). While it does not explicitly mention asking the assistant directly for access or clearly frame a free trial, it broadly satisfies most required elements.

Agent B includes the voice command method (“Alexa, let me try the new Alexa”) and app banner request, which is strong. However, it explicitly states there is “no additional hardware requirement,” which conflicts with the checklist requirement that early access is tied to eligible devices. It also omits reference to an official sign-up/info page and does not clearly mention a free trial model beyond preview access. This results in a Width failure.

Presentation & UX: Agent A is better structured, more comprehensive, and includes clearer segmentation and citations. It reads more like a polished search result. Agent B is concise but less comprehensive and slightly less structured.

Therefore, Agent A provides the better overall user experience with fewer critical omissions.
